content.text:
You are performing the FINAL review of the codebase, focused on a single dimension: Code Quality (code-quality).

Review ALL changes made during this workflow through the lens of this dimension only, and report your findings. The complete diff of the changes is provided below.

## Changes made during this workflow
(No git diff was available — the working directory may not be a git repository, or there are no uncommitted changes against HEAD. Review the working tree directly.)

OUTPUT RULES:
- If this review dimension is NOT applicable to the changeset (for example: a UI/UX review when there are no UI-facing changes, or a security review when there is no security-relevant surface), set applicable=false, explain in notApplicableReason, and return an empty findings array.
- Otherwise set applicable=true and list every real finding, ordered by severity (critical first).
- Rate each finding: low (nit / cosmetic), medium (should fix), high (important), critical (must fix before merge).
- For every finding, write a complete `fixPrompt` that a fixer agent can execute directly and in isolation to resolve it: state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings.
- If there are genuinely no issues for this dimension, return applicable=true with an empty findings array — NEVER fabricate findings.
- Set the `dimension` field of your response to exactly "code-quality".

## Response Format (required)
Respond with ONLY a single valid JSON object matching the schema below.
- No prose, explanations, or commentary before or after the JSON.
- No markdown code fences (```), no headings — output the raw JSON object.
- Do NOT call any tools and do NOT end your turn on a thinking block. Emit the JSON object directly as text.

JSON schema:
{ dimension: The review dimension you assessed (must match the dimension assigned in the prompt), applicable: false when this review dimension does not apply to the changeset (e.g. a UI/UX review with no UI changes). When false, set notApplicableReason and return an empty findings array., notApplicableReason: Required when applicable=false. Explain why this dimension is irrelevant to the current changes. Empty string when applicable=true., summary: Overall assessment for this dimension (what you checked and your conclusion), findings: Array<{ id: Short stable kebab-case identifier, unique within this review (e.g. 'n-plus-one-user-query'), severity: "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical" /* low = nit/cosmetic, medium = should fix, high = important, critical = must fix before merge */, file: Primary file the finding relates to, with line range if helpful (e.g. 'src/auth.ts:42-58'), title: Concise one-line summary of the finding, description: Detailed explanation: what is wrong, why it matters, and the intended correct behavior, fixPrompt: A complete, self-contained prompt that a fixer agent can execute directly to resolve this finding. Must state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings. }> /* Findings ordered by severity, most severe first. Empty when applicable=false or when there are genuinely no issues. */ }

# Software Developer Test Project 2026

## Project Goals

- **Spend a few hours:** Put your best foot forward, submit a project that shows your best work. The evaluation will be code reviews and discussions of your implementation and architecture choices.

- **Download the empty project:** [Here](#). We have started you with an index.html file and an empty controller. When you are done you can zip it up and share it with the team for review either as a download link or zip attachment to an email. If you share the project by email keep in mind the zip has exe files inside it and Gmail and other virus scanners may block the email. To avoid issues, rename the extension to something like `MyProject.CHANGEME`

- **End-to-End Flow that Works:** Implement both server side and client side logic in a primitive end to end flow that runs without errors for a few small features. The key goal is to show a working end to end proof of concept with enough C# and JavaScript code that we can get a feel for your coding style and have some interesting classes to discuss in the code review.

  - **Simple is better:** The less boilerplate junk the better - if you can pick a barebones project type or delete all the default controllers and files you are not using it will help us focus on your code

  - **Focus on Code:** Spend most of your time actually writing code so that we can have a substantial work product to review. For the purpose of this exercise we really need to see original code and are not interested in seeing a lot of framework or template usage.

## File & Directory Browsing Single Page App

The task is to create a web service API that allows users to query the contents of a directory on the web server and a single page web app that can be used to search and browse folders and files. A server side home directory should be configurable via variable.

Client side, the application should be deep-linkable (the state of the UI should be kept in the URL). All of the UI work should be done client side via JavaScript that renders HTML - do not render HTML server side. Styling is not as important for this task, we are more concerned with functionality. Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library).

The goal is to produce a functional proof-of-concept to let us see how you think. It is important to provide a decent body of code, so that we can talk about your logic and choices. It isn't important to make the UI "pretty".

The point of this exercise is to end up with a body of work that is big enough that our team can review it and evaluate your design and coding style and skills.

## Requirements

- Ensure your solution builds in Visual Studio (any version 2022 or newer is acceptable), Rider, VS Code, or command line SDK tools
- Web API that allows Browse and Search Files & Folders and returns JSON
- Deep linkable URL pattern
- SPA (Single Page App using JavaScript)
- Upload/download files from the browser
- Show file and folder counts and sizes for the current view
- Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library)

## Bonus

- Entire component contained in a dialog widget, with a trigger element (button, etc)
- Delete, move, copy files and folders
- Highly performant

Development Diary

1. Ran the Engin harness orchestrator (my own: https://github.com/harms-haus/engin, it uses pi-coding-agent with my own extensions to perform scouting, planning, parallel task implementation, and review) with the "develop" workflow to generate a base to start with.
2. Human Review: Fixed a few issues. Added icon pack (bootstrap). Cleaned up the dialog styling. Added an embedded mode so that the file browser appears in the browser's main page. The button remains and continues to open a dialog version. Added a sample set of files to browse around in.
3. Ran the Engin harness orchestrator with the "improve" workflow to clean up the codebase.
4. Found four distinct issues with the file service: Delete is broken on Windows (I use Arch btw); Upload doesn't check safe destination path; Windows control chars aren't caught by invalid chars; Symlinks allow escaping the sandbox.
5. Added CI/CD: GitHub Actions workflow. Switched to oxlint (faaast). Added prettier. Fixed prettier, linting, and test errors.

content.thinking:
Interesting! There IS a failing test. The test `becomes visible + interactive on row hover and when its menu is open` fails because the CSS rule selector in `app.css` is:

```css
.browse-table tbody tr:hover .row-menu-btn,.row-menu-btn[aria-expanded='true'] {
```

The CSS uses single quotes (`aria-expanded='true'`), but the test's regex is `/\.row-menu-btn\[aria-expanded="true"\]/` which looks for double quotes. Let me confirm:

arguments.command: cd /home/blake/Documents/software/map-large-homework2/.engin/work/1781963759214-develop/worktree && sed -n '1,60p' src/app/css.test.ts 2>&1; echo "---"; sed -n '1,60p' src/app.css.test.ts

content.text: 326:.row-menu-btn[aria-expanded='true'] {

content.thinking:
Confirmed - the failing test is a real bug introduced by this changeset:

The CSS in `src/app.css` uses single quotes:
```css
.browse-table tbody tr:hover .row-menu-btn,
.row-menu-btn[aria-expanded='true'] {
```

The test in `src/app/css.test.ts` (well, `src/app.css.test.ts`) uses double quotes:
```ts
const reveal = ruleDeclarations(/\.row-menu-btn\[aria-expanded="true"\]/);
```

The regex doesn't match the CSS selector due to quote mismatch, so `ruleDeclarations` returns null and the test fails. This is a `medium` or `high` severity issue — it's a failing test in the test suite. Let me check if the test was passing before this PR or if this is a regression.

Wait, both the CSS rule and the test were added in this changeset. So this is a new test that's failing — it's testing a behavior that should pass but doesn't because of a quote mismatch in the test itself (or the CSS).

Either:
1. Fix the CSS to use double quotes
2. Fix the test to accept single quotes

Either way the test must pass.

Now let me think about the actual CSS: with single quotes in the CSS selector `aria-expanded='true'`, that's a valid CSS attribute selector. With `setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'true')` in the DOM, the rendered HTML attribute would be `aria-expanded="true"` with double quotes (in HTML serialization). But CSS attribute selectors with single vs double quotes both work in CSS for matching the rendered attribute. So the CSS itself is correct. The test regex is just too strict.

But more importantly: this is a failing test that was added as part of this PR. That's a real quality issue.

Let me think about severity. A failing test in the suite is `high` (must fix before merge) — but the test isn't even asserting an actual behavior bug, just a CSS string mismatch. Still, the test suite is red, so it should be `medium`-to-`high`.

Let me now look at the code for other quality issues.

1. Looking at the `makeRowMenu` function and its open/close logic:
- The doc comment is good
- `openRowMenu` is module-scoped state — that's fine since only one menu can be open at a time
- However, this state is module-scoped and survives re-renders / unmounts. If `startApp` re-mounts, the `openRowMenu` could still hold a stale reference to a `closeMenu` of a now-removed DOM element. But `closeMenu` just sets `menu.hidden = true` which is harmless even if detached. And on next render the old elements are gone. So calling `closeAllRowMenus()` on a stale reference would just no-op on a detached element. Acceptable.

But there's a subtle issue: when `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` happens during a re-render (e.g., after delete/rerender), the menu's DOM element is removed. If the menu was open before rerender, `openRowMenu` still points to a `closeMenu` that operates on a detached element. The document click/Escape listeners are NOT removed because rerender doesn't call `closeMenu`. So the document listeners linger.

Actually wait, let's trace this:
1. User opens menu on row A → `openRowMenu = { close: closeMenuA }`, doc listeners added
2. User clicks "Delete" → confirm → `api.delete` → `rerender()`
3. `rerender()` → `render()` → `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` → row A's DOM (including menuA) is removed
4. New rows are rendered. But `openRowMenu` still points to `closeMenuA`, and the doc click/keydown listeners added in step 1 are still attached.

So now if the user clicks anywhere, `onDocClickA` fires and calls `closeMenuA`, which:
- Sets `menuA.hidden = true` (harmless, menuA detached)
- Sets btnA's aria-expanded (harmless, detached)
- Removes doc listeners (good — this is the cleanup)
- Clears `openRowMenu` if it still points to closeMenuA

So actually it self-cleans on the next click/Escape after rerender. The lingering listeners fire ONE more time and then clean themselves up. That's a minor leak but not catastrophic.

Actually, this is a real concern though — the doc listeners persist after a rerender until the next click. Worth noting as a `low` to `medium` maintainability issue. The doc comment claims "removed on close rather than leaking across renders" but it actually DOES leak across renders until the next document click.

Let me think again... Actually `closeAllRowMenus()` is only called inside `openMenu`, so on rerender the listeners aren't cleaned. This is a real (minor) leak. Probably `low`-`medium`.

2. Let me check the test issue again more carefully.

Actually let me re-look at the test:
```ts
it('becomes visible + interactive on row hover and when its menu is open', () => {
    // The grouped reveal rule covers both conditions; anchor on the
    // [aria-expanded="true"] selector which is the last one before the brace.
    const reveal = ruleDeclarations(/\.row-menu-btn\[aria-expanded="true"\]/);
    expect(reveal, 'a reveal rule covering [aria-expanded="true"] must exist').not.toBeNull();
```

And the CSS:
```css
.row-menu-btn[aria-expanded='true'] {
```

Yes, the test fails because of the quote mismatch. This is a real bug in the changeset — a test that was added but doesn't pass. This is `high` severity (the test suite is red).

Actually wait, let me verify this is the right severity. Looking at this more carefully:
- It's a test-only issue (the actual app behavior works fine in real browsers, because CSS attribute selectors with single OR double quotes both work)
- But it leaves the test suite red

I'd rate this `high` since a red test suite is a real problem that should be fixed before merge.

Let me also verify by running just that test.

3. Now let me look at the makeRowMenu doc comment more carefully for any "old way" / "previously" comments that should be removed:

"The action-item handler bodies are identical to the former inline buttons (same `window.confirm` / `prompt`, `getApi().delete/move/copy`, `normalizeRelativePath`, and `rerender()` calls) so all behavior is preserved. The items keep `class="btn"` (the `.row-menu .btn` CSS restyles them into menu items) so existing query/characterization assertions hold."

This says "former inline buttons" — this is a timely comment describing past behavior. It's borderline — it's explaining WHY the current code looks the way it does. It does describe "former" behavior but it's also explaining why the items keep `class="btn"`. This is a borderline case; I'd flag it as `low` since it references the past state and will become stale.

Similarly:
"// Menu items — same handlers/attributes as the former inline controls."
"The inline Download/Delete/Move/Copy controls are replaced by a single"

These describe the migration, which is past state. After this PR merges, "former" is meaningless to new readers. `low` severity.

In the CSS comment:
"/* ── Menu items ──
   Flatten the generic .btn styling inside the dropdown.  The elements
   themselves keep class=\"btn\" so query-* / dataset tests that assert
   className work unchanged. */"

This says "work unchanged" which describes the past. Borderline.

4. Now let me look at the test file for any dead/useless tests.

Looking at the render-browse.test.ts tests they look comprehensive and assert real things.

5. Let me check if there are any other issues.

Actually, let me look at one subtle thing. In `closeMenu`:
```js
function closeMenu(): void {
    menu.hidden = true;
    btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
    document.removeEventListener('click', onDocClick);
    document.removeEventListener('keydown', onDocKey);
    if (openRowMenu?.close === closeMenu) {
      openRowMenu = null;
    }
}
```

This sets `menu.hidden = true`. But in the test, when the menu is hidden, clicking a button inside it still works (as the test comment notes). OK.

6. Let me look at the `aria-expanded` value handling. The button uses `setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false')` initially, then `setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'true')` on open. The CSS selector is `[aria-expanded='true']`. This all works fine in real browsers.

7. Let me check if there's an issue with the menu position when right-clicking near the right/bottom edge of the viewport — the menu could overflow off-screen. The CSS uses `position: fixed` with `left` and `top` set to `event.clientX/clientY`. There's no boundary check to flip or clamp the menu if it would overflow the viewport. This is an edge case worth mentioning (`low`).

8. Let me look at the `makeRowMenu` return type. It returns `{ btn, menu, openMenu }`. The `openMenu` is only used by the `contextmenu` handler in `makeBrowseRow`. The `menu` element is appended to `actionsCell` (so it lives in the row). The `btn` is also appended. So all three are used. Good.

Actually wait — `openMenu` accepts `(x, y)` and is called from both:
- The `btn` click handler (with `rect.left, rect.bottom`)
- The `contextmenu` handler (with `event.clientX, event.clientY`)

Both go through `openMenu` which is the same function. Good.

9. The `closeMenu` and `openMenu` are defined inside `makeRowMenu`, so each row has its own closures. This is fine.

10. Naming: `openRowMenu` — the variable name suggests it IS a menu, but it's actually a `{ close }` wrapper. Mildly misleading. Could be `openRowMenuCloser` or similar. `low` severity.

11. There's the issue of the doc comment being very long. The doc comment for `makeRowMenu` is ~25 lines. That's verbose but acceptable for a complex lifecycle.

12. Let me check that the test issue is really the only test failure.

content.text: Let me confirm the failing test and look more carefully at the CSS/test mismatch:

content.thinking:
Confirmed. The CSS uses single quotes (`aria-expanded='true'`) while the test regex uses double quotes (`aria-expanded="true"`). This causes the failing test.

Let me now look at the render-browse.ts docstring header for any issues, and look at the makeRowMenu return type usage.

Let me also check the `dev-diary.md` and the task to see if there are any extra files in the changeset.

Let me also check whether `aria-haspopup="true"` should be `aria-haspopup="menu"` (a11y best practice). The value "true" is a legacy alias for "menu". Modern best practice is `aria-haspopup="menu"`. But "true" is still valid per the spec. Minor nit.

content.thinking:
Let me check the `beforeEach` in the new test block. It dispatches an Escape keydown to close any open menu. But this only works because `onDocKey` is registered on `document`. If a prior test left a menu open, this should close it. However, this only handles keydown-based close. If a prior test left listeners dangling, the beforeEach cleans them up via the Escape key. That's a reasonable approach.

But wait — the `beforeEach` only applies within the `describe('row action menu')` block. Other test blocks (like the Actions-column behavior tests) don't have this cleanup. If a row-action-menu test leaves a menu open, then a test in another block runs... actually the tests within `row action menu` block all await setupCleared() which mounts fresh, but the module-scoped `openRowMenu` persists across tests. So the beforeEach is needed. OK.

Actually, the issue is that `setupCleared()` re-mounts the app, but doesn't reset `openRowMenu`. So if a test leaves `openRowMenu` pointing to a stale closer, the next test's click might trigger `closeAllRowMenus()` which calls a stale closer. The stale closer operates on a detached menu (harmless) but removes doc listeners that were already removed (no-op). OK.

Now let me think about whether there's a correctness issue with the render race. After `rerender()` is called from an action handler, `render()` clears `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''`. If a menu was open, its DOM is removed. The `openRowMenu` still points to the old closer. The doc listeners are still attached. The next document click will trigger `onDocClickA` (the old one) which calls `closeMenuA` → removes itself. So one extra cleanup happens. Minor leak.

Actually this is more subtle. Let me trace:
1. User opens menu on row A → `openRowMenu = { close: closeMenuA }`, doc listeners `onDocClickA` and `onDocKeyA` added
2. User clicks "Delete" → confirm → `api.delete` resolves → `rerender()`
3. `rerender()` → `render()` → `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` → row A's DOM is removed
4. New rows rendered. `openRowMenu` still points to closeMenuA. Doc listeners still attached.
5. User opens menu on row B → `openMenuB` calls `closeAllRowMenus()` → calls closeMenuA → removes listeners A, sets openRowMenu=null → then adds listeners B.

So actually it self-cleans on the NEXT menu open OR the next click. OK, this is acceptable.

But the doc comment claims: "removed on close rather than leaking across renders or rapid open/close cycles." This claim is partially inaccurate — they DO leak across renders until the next open or click. But this is a minor doc inaccuracy.

Let me focus on the key issues to report:

1. **HIGH/CRITICAL**: Failing test — `src/app.css.test.ts > becomes visible + interactive on row hover and when its menu is open`. The CSS uses single quotes `aria-expanded='true'` while the test regex looks for double quotes `aria-expanded="true"`. This leaves the test suite red.

3. **LOW/medium**: Viewport boundary edge case — menu positioned at `clientX/clientY` with no clamping could overflow the viewport on right-click near right/bottom edges.

4. **LOW**: `aria-haspopup="true"` — modern best practice is `aria-haspopup="menu"` for a dropdown menu. "true" is a legacy alias.

5. **LOW**: Misleading name `openRowMenu` — holds a `{ close }` wrapper, not a menu. Minor.

6. **LOW/medium**: Module-scoped `openRowMenu` listener cleanup gap — on rerender with a menu open, the doc click/keydown listeners linger until the next click/Escape or next menu open. The doc comment claims they don't leak across renders, which is slightly inaccurate.

Then in `src/app/render-browse.test.ts`, update the two places that assert the legacy value to match: the test title at approximately line 588 (`... aria-haspopup=\"true\" ...`) and the assertion at approximately line 597 (`expect(btn.getAttribute('aria-haspopup')).toBe('true');`) — change the expected value to `'menu'`. Run `npx vitest run src/app/render-browse.test.ts` and confirm all tests pass."},{"id":"menu-not-clamped-to-viewport","severity":"low","file":"src/app/dom-builders.ts:241-247","title":"Row menu positioned at raw clientX/clientY with no viewport-edge clamping","description":"`openMenu(x, y)` sets `menu.style.left = x + 'px'` and `menu.style.top = y + 'px'` directly from the cursor / button-bottom coordinates. Because the menu is `position: fixed` with `min-width: 140px`, a right-click (or a ⋮ click on a button near the right edge) at large `clientX` or `clientY` will push the menu off the right or bottom edge of the viewport, making some or all action items unreachable/unselectable. There is no clamping against `window.innerWidth`/`window.innerHeight` minus the menu's measured width/height. Consider flipping or clamping the position when it would overflow.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app/dom-builders.ts`, in the `openMenu(x, y)` function inside `makeRowMenu`, after setting `menu.hidden = false` (so the element is laid out and measurable), clamp the menu's position so it cannot overflow the viewport. Before assigning `menu.style.left`/`menu.style.top`, measure the menu with `const rect = menu.getBoundingClientRect();` and adjust: if `x + rect.width > window.innerWidth`, set `x = Math.max(0, window.innerWidth - rect.width - 4)`; likewise if `y + rect.height > window.innerHeight`, set `y = Math.max(0, window.innerHeight - rect.height - 4)`. Keep the existing `menu.style.left = x + 'px'` / `menu.style.top = y + 'px'` assignments using the clamped values, and keep the rest of the function (aria-expanded flip, document listeners, `openRowMenu` assignment) unchanged. The existing tests in `src/app/render-browse.test.ts` that assert `menu.style.left` matches the raw `clientX` (e.g. the contextmenu test expecting `'137px'`) use coordinates well inside the viewport, so they will still pass with clamping applied; run `npx vitest run src/app/render-browse.test.ts` to confirm."}]}
